sourceTitle: Use Notion’s Data Retention settings to keep your team compliant & restore content

questions.question: After a Notion page is deleted, which three locations can it occupy as dictated by data retention settings?

options.text: Draft, Published, Archived
options.explanation: These are not stages in Notion’s deletion workflow.

options.text: Trash, Retained pages, Purged pages
options.explanation: Correct – a deleted page first goes to Trash, then to a Retained period, and finally is Purged.

options.text: Shared, Private, Deleted
options.explanation: These describe access levels, not post‑deletion states.

options.text: Active, Inactive, Archived
options.explanation: These are not part of Notion’s data‑retention lifecycle.

questions.question: Who has the ability to permanently restore a page that is currently in the retention period?

options.text: Any workspace member
options.explanation: Regular members can only restore pages while they are still in Trash, not during retention.

options.text: Guest users
options.explanation: Guests have limited permissions and cannot restore retained pages.

options.text: Enterprise workspace owners
options.explanation: Only Enterprise owners (or admins) can restore pages once they have entered the retention stage.

options.text: Notion support staff
options.explanation: Support cannot restore pages on behalf of a workspace; the owner must do it.

questions.question: What is the result of a page being "purged" in Notion?

options.text: It moves back to the Trash automatically
options.explanation: Purged pages are removed permanently; they never return to Trash.

options.text: It can still be recovered via Content Search
options.explanation: Purged pages are no longer searchable or recoverable.

options.text: It is permanently removed and cannot be restored
options.explanation: Correct – once purged, a page is gone forever.

options.text: It becomes a template for future pages
options.explanation: Purged pages are deleted, not turned into templates.

questions.question: A company that must comply with HIPAA may adjust Notion’s data retention settings primarily to:

options.text: Increase overall storage capacity
options.explanation: Retention settings affect how long data is kept, not storage capacity directly.

options.text: Meet regulatory data‑retention requirements
options.explanation: HIPAA mandates specific retention periods; custom settings help meet those legal obligations.

options.text: Enable faster page loading times
options.explanation: Retention periods do not impact page performance.

options.text: Facilitate sharing pages with external partners
options.explanation: Sharing permissions are unrelated to retention policies.

questions.question: By default, how many days does a deleted page remain in the Trash before it moves to the retention period?

options.text: 7 days
options.explanation: The default is longer than a week.

options.text: 30 days
options.explanation: Correct – Notion’s default Trash duration is 30 days.

options.text: 90 days
options.explanation: 90 days is not the default; it can be set manually.

options.text: 365 days
options.explanation: One year is the maximum you can configure, not the default.

questions.question: Which restore option moves a deleted page into the restorer’s private pages instead of its original location?

options.text: Restore page
options.explanation: This option returns the page to its original spot, preserving original access.

options.text: Restore to your private pages
options.explanation: Correct – this places the page in the restorer’s private area, keeping it confidential.

options.text: Permanently delete
options.explanation: Permanently deleting removes the page, not restores it.

options.text: Move to workspace
options.explanation: There is no "move to workspace" restore action in Notion.

questions.question: An Enterprise workspace owner wants to locate a page that is in the retention period. Which navigation path should they use?

options.text: Click the Trash icon in the left‑hand sidebar
options.explanation: Trash only shows items still within the Trash window, not retained pages.

options.text: Open Settings & members → Workspaces → Content Search
options.explanation: Content Search under Settings & members lets owners find retained pages.

options.text: Email Notion support for a manual lookup
options.explanation: Support cannot retrieve retained pages on behalf of a workspace.

options.text: Use the global search bar at the top of the sidebar
options.explanation: The global search does not include pages that have left the Trash.

questions.question: Setting both the Trash and Retention periods to the maximum of 10 years will:

options.text: Reduce storage costs while potentially violating long‑term regulations
options.explanation: Longer retention typically increases storage usage, not reduces it.

options.text: Increase storage costs but ensure compliance with long‑term regulatory requirements
options.explanation: Storing data for up to 10 years raises storage demand, yet satisfies regulations that demand long retention.

options.text: Have no impact on storage costs and automatically encrypt all retained data
options.explanation: Retention length does affect storage; encryption is independent of retention settings.

options.text: Disable the ability to restore pages after five years
options.explanation: Pages remain restorable throughout the entire retention period, regardless of length.

questions.question: When a block is deleted from a Notion page, where does its history reside and how is it treated during the page’s retention period?

options.text: Deleted blocks are permanently erased immediately
options.explanation: Blocks are not instantly removed; they stay in page history.

options.text: They are stored in a separate archive and are not retained with the page
options.explanation: Blocks remain tied to the page’s history, not a separate archive.

options.text: They remain in the page’s history and are retained alongside the page during retention
options.explanation: Correct – block history is part of the page’s version history and follows the same retention rules.

options.text: They become new independent pages
options.explanation: Deleted blocks do not transform into separate pages.

questions.question: A team wants regular pages restored to their original location after accidental deletion, but legal documents must stay confidential. Which combination of restore actions should they use?

options.text: Always use “Restore page” for all pages
options.explanation: Legal documents would become visible to the original collaborators, breaking confidentiality.

options.text: Use “Restore to your private pages” for regular pages and “Restore page” for sensitive ones
options.explanation: This reverses the intended confidentiality handling.

options.text: Use “Restore page” for regular pages and “Restore to your private pages” for sensitive legal pages
options.explanation: Correct – this keeps legal documents private while returning normal pages to where they belong.

options.text: Delete the pages again to keep them confidential
options.explanation: Deleting again would move them back to Trash, risking loss if the retention period expires.
